(October 21, 1928 – November 21, 2013) was an American professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") player. One of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s first [power forwards](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") in the 1950s, he was known for his tenacious defense and durability, playing 699 out of a possible 704 games during his career.[[1]](#cite_note-wpost-1) He was a six-time All-Star and four-time Second Team All-Pro, and was inducted into the NAIA Basketball Hall of Fame[[2]](#cite_note-sportingnews-2) and the sport's [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 1995.

**Arild Verner Agerskov Mikkelsen** (October 21, 1928 – November 21, 2013) was an American professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") player. One of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s first [power forwards](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") in the 1950s, he was known for his tenacious defense and durability, playing 699 out of a possible 704 games during his career.[[1]](#cite_note-wpost-1) He was a six-time All-Star and four-time Second Team All-Pro, and was inducted into the NAIA Basketball Hall of Fame[[2]](#cite_note-sportingnews-2) and the sport's [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 1995.

Along with fellow Hall of Famers [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan") and [Slater Martin](/wiki/Slater_Martin "Slater Martin") he was a key part of four championships with the [Minneapolis Lakers](/wiki/Minneapolis_Lakers "Minneapolis Lakers"), today's [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers").

Mikkelsen was also an All-American in college, and earned a master's degree from the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Mikkelsen was born in [Parlier, California](/wiki/Parlier,_California "Parlier, California") and was raised in the Danish-American community of [Askov, Minnesota](/wiki/Askov,_Minnesota "Askov, Minnesota"). His father, Michael, was an immigrant from Denmark who became a Lutheran pastor in Askov.[[3]](#cite_note-goldstein-3)

## College career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: College career")]

Mikkelsen entered [Hamline University](/wiki/Hamline_University "Hamline University") in [Saint Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota") on a basketball scholarship at the age of 16. In his senior year, Mikkelsen led NCAA Division II in field goal percentage.[[2]](#cite_note-sportingnews-2) Hamline won the [1949 NAIA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/1949_NAIA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1949 NAIA Division I men's basketball tournament")[[3]](#cite_note-goldstein-3) and Mikkelsen was voted an All-American. He would later receive a master's degree in psychology from the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota").

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Professional career")]

### Minneapolis Lakers (1949-1960)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Minneapolis Lakers (1949-1960)")]

Mikkelsen played with [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan") and [Jim Pollard](/wiki/Jim_Pollard "Jim Pollard") in the frontcourt of the [Minneapolis Lakers](/wiki/Minneapolis_Lakers "Minneapolis Lakers"). The Lakers won four NBA titles during Mikkelsen's career.[[1]](#cite_note-wpost-1) Mikkelsen played in six [NBA All-Star Games](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association_All-Star_Game "National Basketball Association All-Star Game") and was named to the All-NBA Second Team four times in his career.

Mikkelsen ended his career after ten seasons in the NBA in 1959, having played in 699 of a possible 704 regular-season games. He led the NBA in both personal fouls and [fouling out of games](/wiki/Personal_foul_(basketball)#Fouling_out "Personal foul (basketball)") for three straight seasons during his career, and finished his career with 10,063 points scored. Mikkelsen still holds the league record for career games fouling out with 127, which he did in only 631 games (the NBA did not record that statistic until his second season).

## Honors and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Honors and awards")]

In 1956, Mikkelsen was inducted into the NAIA Basketball Hall of Fame.[[2]](#cite_note-sportingnews-2)

Mikkelsen was inducted into the [Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") in 1995 along with Laker coach [John Kundla](/wiki/John_Kundla "John Kundla").[[3]](#cite_note-goldstein-3)

In 2002, during halftime of a Lakers/Timberwolves game, Mikkelsen and fellow Hall of Fame teammates [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan"), [Slater Martin](/wiki/Slater_Martin "Slater Martin"), Arlee Pollard (widow of [Jim Pollard](/wiki/Jim_Pollard "Jim Pollard")), [Clyde Lovellette](/wiki/Clyde_Lovellette "Clyde Lovellette") and Coach [John Kundla](/wiki/John_Kundla "John Kundla") were each presented with championship rings. The Minneapolis players received the same rings provided by the NBA to the champion Los Angeles Lakers that same year.

## Coaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Coaching")]

Mikkelsen was involved with insurance when he was asked to do general managing for the [Minnesota Muskies](/wiki/Minnesota_Muskies "Minnesota Muskies") in April of 1968.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He later coached and was general manager of the [Minnesota Pipers](/wiki/Minnesota_Pipers "Minnesota Pipers") of the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Personal life and family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vern_Mikkelsen&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life and family")]

Mikkelsen's wife Jean died in 2002 after 47 years of marriage. Their two sons are named Tom and John. In 2006 a biography was published by John Egan titled *The Vern Mikkelsen Story*.

Mikkelsen died on November 21, 2013, in [Wayzata, Minnesota](/wiki/Wayzata,_Minnesota "Wayzata, Minnesota") surrounded by his family.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
